title: Jerk fish
subtitle: Cod fillets in Jamaican jerk marinade, grilled or baked
excerpt: Thick fish fillets marinated briefly in scotch bonnet, allspice, spring onion, and thyme, then cooked at high heat until the surface chars slightly and the flesh flakes. Fast enough for a weeknight.
sourceNotes: Jerk seasoning is applied to fish as well as chicken and pork in Jamaican cooking, particularly at beach stalls where whole fish are cooked over pimento wood. This recipe uses cod fillet as a widely available white fish that takes the seasoning well. The marinade is shorter than for jerk chicken (20–30 minutes is sufficient for fish; longer and the acid begins to cure the flesh). Red snapper or sea bream would be more traditional if available.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The jerk marinade can be made up to 5 days ahead and stored in the fridge. Marinate the fish no more than 30 minutes before cooking.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan oven, or cook under a hot grill

content.text: Jerk works well on fish for the same reason it works on chicken: the paste, applied to a surface that will cook fast and hot, becomes a lacquered crust while the interior stays moist. The difference is the timing. Chicken needs a full overnight in the marinade to let the flavours penetrate; fish needs only 20–30 minutes. Leave it longer and the lime and scotch bonnet begin to cure the flesh, changing its texture before it ever reaches the heat.

Cod fillet holds up to the heat and the seasoning better than most white fish. Use thick fillets (at least 2.5 cm) so they can take a hot oven without cooking through before the crust has formed. A hot grill works equally well if you prefer; the slight charring from direct heat is closer to the pimento wood smoke of the Jamaican original.

items.prepNote: thick fillets, skin on — or use sea bream or red snapper if available
deseeded for moderate heat, whole for full heat

content.text: Put the {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet, {{spring-onion}} spring onions, {{garlic}} of garlic, {{ginger-root}} of ginger, {{thyme-fresh}} of fresh thyme, {{allspice}} of allspice, {{cinnamon-ground}} of cinnamon, {{dark-brown-sugar}} of dark brown sugar, {{soy-sauce-dark}} of dark soy sauce, the juice of the {{lime}} lime, and {{vegetable-oil}} of oil into a food processor. Blitz to a rough paste. Taste — it should be hot, fragrant, and slightly sweet.

Pat the {{cod-fillet}} fish fillets dry with kitchen paper. Score the skin of each fillet twice with a sharp knife — this stops the fish from curling in the heat and lets the marinade penetrate. Rub the jerk paste all over the fillets, getting into the scores. Leave for 20–30 minutes at room temperature. Preheat the oven to 220°C fan. Place the fillets skin-side down on a lightly oiled roasting tray. Roast for 12–15 minutes until the surface is slightly charred at the edges and the flesh flakes when pressed at the thickest point.

items.cause: Cooked too long; lifting before the skin crisped and released
items.fix: Use a fish slice and get it underneath the skin. Let the fish cook until it lifts cleanly from the tray — forcing it up early tears the flesh.

items.symptom: Jerk paste too hot to handle
items.fix: Deseed the scotch bonnet before blitzing. Wash hands thoroughly after handling and avoid touching your face.
Pat fillets completely dry before applying the paste. Surface moisture prevents adhesion.

items.cause: Oven not hot enough; fish too crowded on the tray
items.fix: The oven must be fully preheated to 220°C. Leave space between fillets — crowding steams rather than roasts. A rack under the grill for 3–4 minutes finishes the crust.

content.text: Jerk fish is beach food in Jamaica: whole red snapper or parrotfish, butterflied and cooked over pimento wood coals, the smoke and spice inseparable. The Jamaican beach cook would scoff at cod, but the principle transfers well. Serve with rice and peas, festival dumplings, and something cold. The jerk paste here also works brushed onto prawns or mackerel fillets if you want a quicker or cheaper version of the same idea.
